summary: American minimalist composer (born 1937), the most-performed living American composer. Known for repetitive arpeggiated structures and additive rhythmic processes. Student of Nadia Boulanger. Created the Philip Glass Ensemble. Major works include the Portrait Trilogy operas (Einstein on the Beach, Satyagraha, Akhnaten), the film score Koyaanisqatsi, the symphonies Low and Heroes (after David Bowie albums), and the album Glassworks.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: American minimalist composer, widely considered the most-performed living American composer. Student of Nadia Boulanger and Vincent Persichetti (who also taught him at Juilliard). Juilliard classmate of Steve Reich. Leads the Philip Glass Ensemble, for which he composed Music in Twelve Parts (organ is the only instrument that plays in every section). Worked closely with the Mabou Mines theater company. Uses a characteristic ensemble of four saxophones, two flutes, three electric organs, and female voice. Moondog (the 'Viking of 6th Avenue') was a major influence. Music released primarily on Nonesuch Records. Collaborated with Ravi Shankar on the album Passages (1990). Wrote three chamber operas based on Jean Cocteau scripts (including Orphée). The play Philip Glass Buys a Loaf of Bread by David Ives parodies his minimalist style with lines like 'Go! Go! Go! Time! Time! Time!' Wikipedia

works.name: Einstein on the Beach
works.description: Five-hour opera with no conventional plot, created in collaboration with director Robert Wilson. First in Glass's Portrait Trilogy. Divided into three acts — 'Train,' 'Trial,' and 'Field/Spaceship' — separated by five 'Knee Plays' (intermezzos in which solfege syllables are chanted and numbers counted). An electric organ and chorus chanting an increasing sequence of numbers perform during the first and final knee plays. A solo violinist (Robert Brown in the original Avignon production) is dressed as the title figure (Einstein). The audience is welcome to leave and return at any time. Includes text by Christopher Knowles ('Prematurely Air-Conditioned Supermarket' by Lucinda Childs, read from a bed) and choreography by Lucinda Childs. Samuel M. Johnson's texts about lovers appear throughout. A bus driver narrates a story of love at the end of one Knee Play. The 1984 revival was choreographed by Lucinda Childs. The opera's penultimate scene is described as 'apocalyptic' in Glass's autobiography Words Without Music. Wikipedia

works.name: Satyagraha
works.description: Second opera in the Portrait Trilogy, about Mahatma Gandhi's early years in South Africa. Sung in Sanskrit. Libretto by Constance DeJong. Third act contains the 'Newcastle March.' Miss Schlesen and Kallenbach sing a duet describing 'how the saints attained success.' Wikipedia

works.name: Akhnaten
works.description: Third opera in the Portrait Trilogy, about the monotheist pharaoh Akhenaten. Title role is a countertenor (who thus sings higher than his wife Nefertiti, a contralto); Anthony Roth Costanzo wore gold leaf on his bald head in the 2019 Met production (directed by Phelim McDermott, with J'Nai Bridges as Nefertiti). A solo trumpet frequently accompanies the countertenor title character. Celesta doubles the synthesizer; notably lacks violins, giving strings a stronger tone. An offstage chorus sings Psalm 104. The Hymn to the Sun ('Thou dost appear beautiful') in Act II is always performed in the audience's native language; described as a long A major and A minor aria. A tour guide declares 'there is nothing left' of Akhenaten's temples in Act III. The epilogue jumps thousands of years into the future to depict tourists walking among ruins as two ghosts wordlessly sing and join a funeral procession from the first act. Phelim McDermott's staging uses juggling background performers. Wikipedia

works.name: Koyaanisqatsi (Film Score)
works.description: Score for Godfrey Reggio's 1982 experimental film, distributed by Francis Ford Coppola. The title is a Hopi word meaning 'life out of balance.' Bass vocalists chant the five-syllable title repeatedly in an ominous descending four-note organ ostinato while the camera slowly zooms out from cave paintings. The track 'Pruit Igoe' plays during the destruction of the Pruitt–Igoe housing complex. Cinematographer Ron Fricke shot a rocket launch, the Great Gallery at Horseshoe Canyon, and the Pruitt-Igoe demolition; Fricke later created the similar film Baraka. Part of a trilogy of Reggio films (followed by Powaqqatsi and Naqoyqatsi). Music from the second film's 'anthems' and Reggio's short Anima Mundi appears in the 'realization' scene from The Truman Show. Wikipedia

works.name: Low Symphony and Heroes Symphony
works.description: Two symphonies based on David Bowie's Berlin Trilogy albums: Low (1992) and Heroes (1996). A third symphony based on Bowie's Lodger (Symphony No. 12) premiered in 2019. The Low Symphony's first movement is titled 'Subterraneans.' Fourth symphony has six movements and derives material from a song containing 'I wish you could swim / like the dolphins.' Wikipedia

works.name: String Quartets
works.description: Nine string quartets in total. No. 2 ('Company') was incidental music for a Mabou Mines production of Beckett's novella Company; Glass describes it as a 'prose poem.' No. 3 ('Mishima') was extracted from the film score to Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters (performed by Kronos Quartet). No. 4 was written in memory of the artist Brian Buczak, commissioned by Geoffrey Hendricks, premiered at the Hauser Gallery. No. 5 contains movements 'Body Building' and 'Blood Oath.' A string sextet is a reduction of Glass's Third Symphony. Most recent quartet (No. 9, 2022) was inspired by King Lear. The Kronos Quartet recorded many of these works. Wikipedia

works.name: Glassworks
works.description: 1982 album of six-movement chamber music, intended to popularize Glass's work. Lead-off track 'Opening' consists of a solo piano continually repeating four-bar passages of hemiola. Also includes the movements 'Floe,' 'Island,' 'Rubric,' and 'Facades.' Glass created a special 'Sony Walkman mix' for the album. Wikipedia

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Other notable works by Philip Glass clued in quizbowl. 1000 Airplanes on the Roof: one-person opera (collaboration with David Henry Hwang) about a man who claims to have met aliens; largely a monologue by a character 'M' on a holographic set. The Perfect American: opera depicting Walt Disney on his deathbed, discussing the cryogenic preservation conspiracy. The Trial: 2014 opera based on Kafka's unfinished novel, libretto by Christopher Hampton. In the Penal Colony: 'pocket opera' based on Kafka, libretto by Rudy Wurlitzer; JoAnne Akalaitis added Kafka himself as a character. The Voyage: opera featuring Christopher Columbus, commissioned for the 500th anniversary of Columbus's voyage. Appomattox: opera about the end of the American Civil War. Hydrogen Jukebox: chamber opera; title comes from a phrase in Allen Ginsberg's 'Howl.' Galileo Galilei: opera ending with Galileo viewing his father's opera with the Duchess. The Photographer: 1982 opera about Eadweard Muybridge's acquittal for murder. Songs from Liquid Days: album with lyrics by Suzanne Vega (movement 'Freezing') and Paul Simon. Metamorphosis: five piano pieces on the album Solo Piano (followed by 'Mad Rush'); Pearl Jam typically opens concerts with 'Metamorphosis Two.' Mad Rush: piano piece inspired by the Dalai Lama's visit to New York; largely hemiolic. 20 Piano Etudes: completed 2014; first six written for the 50th birthday of conductor Dennis Russell Davies; Maki Namekawa recorded the complete cycle. Violin Concerto No. 2: traditional cadenzas replaced with a 'Prologue' and three 'Songs.' Piano Concerto No. 2 ('After Lewis and Clark'): second-movement duet features Native American flutist R. Carlos Nakai. Symphony No. 7 (Toltec): Mesoamerican-inspired; uses wood-blocks and glockenspiels. Concerto Fantasy for Two Timpanists: timpanists must play with their hands for a higher pitch.

comprehensive_summary:
Philip Glass (born 1937) is an American minimalist composer widely recognized as the most-performed living American composer. He studied with Nadia Boulanger in Paris and with Vincent Persichetti at Juilliard, where he was a classmate of Steve Reich. A major influence on Glass was Moondog, the 'Viking of 6th Avenue.' He leads the Philip Glass Ensemble, for which he composed Music in Twelve Parts — a three-hour work originally misunderstood to have twelve movements when Glass actually meant twelve musical lines; the organ is the only instrument that plays in every section. Glass has long association with the Mabou Mines theater company (for whom he wrote Music for Voices) and collaborated extensively with the Kronos Quartet, which recorded his string quartets and various film scores. He collaborated with Ravi Shankar on the album Passages (1990). Much of his music has been released on Nonesuch Records. He also wrote a Concerto for Saxophone Quartet for the Rascher Quartet, whose third movement never rises above mezzo forte.

Glass's most celebrated achievement is his 'Portrait Trilogy' of operas about three world-historical figures: Einstein on the Beach (1976, Albert Einstein), Satyagraha (1979, Gandhi), and Akhnaten (1983, the Egyptian pharaoh Akhenaten). Einstein on the Beach, created in collaboration with director Robert Wilson, is a five-hour opera with no conventional plot. Audiences are welcome to leave and return at will. It is divided into three acts — 'Train,' 'Trial,' and 'Field/Spaceship' — separated by five 'Knee Plays.' An electric organ and chorus chanting an increasing sequence of numbers perform during the first and final knee plays; the opera ends with the choir singing number patterns in German. Robert Brown portrayed the solo violinist dressed as Einstein in the original Avignon production. Text contributions came from Christopher Knowles and Lucinda Childs, including the section 'Prematurely Air-Conditioned Supermarket' read by Childs from a bed. Samuel M. Johnson's texts about lovers appear throughout. The opera opens with a white square highlighting two figures seated at desks. Glass's autobiography Words Without Music describes the penultimate scene as 'apocalyptic.' The 1984 revival was choreographed by Lucinda Childs. A Los Angeles Opera production staged dancers in suspenders on scaffolding in front of LED circles.

Satyagraha, with a libretto by Constance DeJong, is sung entirely in Sanskrit, and its third act contains the 'Newcastle March'; Miss Schlesen and Kallenbach sing a duet about 'how the saints attained success.' Akhnaten features a countertenor in the title role, meaning Akhenaten sings higher notes than his contralto wife Nefertiti; a solo trumpet frequently accompanies the countertenor. Anthony Roth Costanzo wore gold leaf on his bald head in the 2019 Met production (directed by Phelim McDermott, with J'Nai Bridges as Nefertiti). The opera lacks violins (the celesta doubles the synthesizer), its offstage chorus sings Psalm 104, and the 'Hymn to the Sun' ('Thou dost appear beautiful') from Act II — a long A major and A minor aria — is always performed in the audience's native language. The libretto uses Hebrew, Akkadian, and ancient Egyptian. A tour guide in Act III declares 'there is nothing left' of Akhenaten's temples. The epilogue jumps thousands of years into the future to depict tourists walking among ruins as two ghosts wordlessly sing and join a funeral procession from Act I. Phelim McDermott's celebrated staging uses juggling performers in the background.

Glass's film scores are equally prominent in quizbowl. Koyaanisqatsi (1982), directed by Godfrey Reggio and distributed by Francis Ford Coppola, opens with an ominous descending four-note organ ostinato while bass vocalists repeatedly chant the five-syllable Hopi title ('life out of balance') as the camera zooms out from cave paintings. Cinematographer Ron Fricke shot a rocket launch, the Great Gallery at Horseshoe Canyon, and the Pruitt-Igoe demolition; Fricke later created the similar film Baraka. The track 'Pruit Igoe' accompanies the demolition of the Pruitt–Igoe housing complex. Shots depict a satellite photo of LA dissolving into a close-up of a microchip, alongside timelapses of traffic and clouds. It is the first in a Reggio trilogy; music from Powaqqatsi and Reggio's short Anima Mundi appears in the 'realization' scene from The Truman Show. The trilogy inspired the first Grand Theft Auto IV trailer. Glass also scored Martin Scorsese's Kundun, received an Academy Award nomination for The Hours, and collaborated with Errol Morris on A Brief History of Time and The Thin Blue Line (Morris once told Glass his music wasn't repetitive enough).

Glass composed two celebrated symphonies based on David Bowie's Berlin Trilogy albums: the Low Symphony and the Heroes Symphony. Symphony No. 12, based on Bowie's Lodger, premiered in 2019. Glass has composed nine string quartets; No. 2 ('Company') was incidental music for a Mabou Mines production of Samuel Beckett's Company; Glass calls the novella a 'prose poem.' No. 3 ('Mishima') was extracted from his Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters soundtrack. No. 4 was commissioned by Geoffrey Hendricks, premiered at the Hauser Gallery, and was written in memory of the artist Brian Buczak. The most recent (No. 9, 2022) was inspired by King Lear. The Kronos Quartet has recorded many of them.

Glassworks (1982) is a six-movement album whose lead-off track 'Opening' consists of a solo piano repeating four-bar passages of hemiola; other movements include 'Floe,' 'Island,' 'Rubric,' and 'Facades,' with a special 'Sony Walkman mix.' Glass's 20 Piano Etudes (completed 2014) had their first six written for conductor Dennis Russell Davies's 50th birthday; Maki Namekawa recorded the complete cycle. His Violin Concerto No. 2 replaces traditional cadenzas with a 'Prologue' and three 'Songs.' Piano Concerto No. 2 ('After Lewis and Clark') features a second-movement duet with Native American flutist R. Carlos Nakai.

Other important operas: 1000 Airplanes on the Roof is a one-person opera about alien abduction, created with David Henry Hwang; The Perfect American depicts Walt Disney on his deathbed; The Trial (2014) adapted Kafka with a libretto by Christopher Hampton; In the Penal Colony is a 'pocket opera' based on Kafka with libretto by Rudy Wurlitzer (JoAnne Akalaitis added Kafka as a character); The Voyage features Christopher Columbus; Appomattox depicts the end of the Civil War; Hydrogen Jukebox is a chamber opera whose title comes from Ginsberg's 'Howl'; Galileo Galilei ends with Galileo viewing his father's opera with the Duchess; The Photographer (1982) examines Eadweard Muybridge's murder acquittal; three chamber operas are based on Jean Cocteau scripts (including Orphée). Songs from Liquid Days set lyrics by Suzanne Vega (in 'Freezing') with a string quartet. The Solo Piano album includes five 'Metamorphosis' pieces (Pearl Jam traditionally opens concerts with 'Metamorphosis Two') and the largely hemiolic Mad Rush (inspired by the Dalai Lama's visit to New York). The play Philip Glass Buys a Loaf of Bread by David Ives (in the anthology All in the Timing) parodies Glass's style with lines like 'Go! Go! Go! Time! Time! Time!'

cards.front: Composer: American minimalist, most-performed living American composer; leads the Philip Glass Ensemble; studied with Nadia Boulanger and Vincent Persichetti
Composer: composed Music in Twelve Parts for his ensemble — the title refers to 12 musical lines, not movements, after a friend's misunderstanding
Composer: the David Ives play Philip Glass Buys a Loaf of Bread (in All in the Timing) parodies his minimalist style with lines like 'Go! Go! Go! Time! Time! Time!'
Composer: nominated for an Academy Award for The Hours; also scored Kundun (Scorsese) and collaborated with Errol Morris on A Brief History of Time and The Thin Blue Line
Opera: five-hour work with no plot; audience may come and go freely; created with Robert Wilson; first in the Portrait Trilogy
Opera: divided into acts 'Train,' 'Trial,' and 'Field/Spaceship' separated by five 'Knee Plays' in which chorus members count numbers and chant solfege syllables
Opera: a solo violinist is dressed as Einstein throughout; section 'Prematurely Air-Conditioned Supermarket' uses text by Lucinda Childs read from a bed
Opera: about Gandhi; sung entirely in Sanskrit; third act contains the 'Newcastle March'; second in the Portrait Trilogy
Opera: about the pharaoh Akhenaten; title role is a countertenor (sings higher than his contralto wife Nefertiti); offstage chorus sings Psalm 104; lacks violins; third in the Portrait Trilogy
Opera: Act II 'Hymn to the Sun' ('Thou dost appear beautiful') is always performed in the audience's native language; libretto uses Hebrew, Akkadian, and ancient Egyptian
Opera: Phelim McDermott's staging uses juggling background performers; Act III features a tour guide declaring 'there is nothing left' of the title figure's temples

Film score: Godfrey Reggio film; title means 'life out of balance' in Hopi; bass vocalists chant the five-syllable title over an ominous descending organ ostinato; 'Pruit Igoe' track plays during the demolition of the Pruitt–Igoe housing complex
Symphonies: Low Symphony and Heroes Symphony are based on David Bowie's Berlin Trilogy albums; Symphony No. 12 (based on Lodger) premiered in 2019
String Quartet: No. 2 ('Company') based on Beckett's novella Company; No. 3 ('Mishima') extracted from Mishima film score; nine total; Kronos Quartet recorded them
Album: six-movement chamber work including 'Floe,' 'Island,' 'Rubric,' and 'Facades'; Glass created a special 'Sony Walkman mix' for it
Opera: one-person work about a man named 'M' who claims to have met aliens; set on a holographic stage; created with David Henry Hwang
Composer: Pearl Jam typically opens concerts with his piano piece 'Metamorphosis Two'; Mad Rush was inspired by the Dalai Lama's visit to New York
Composer: collaborated with Ravi Shankar on the album Passages (1990); Moondog was a major influence on him; music released on Nonesuch Records
Composer: wrote three chamber operas based on Jean Cocteau scripts (including Orphee); also wrote a Concerto for Saxophone Quartet for the Rascher Quartet

Opera: electric organ and chorus chanting increasing numbers perform during its first and final 'knee plays'; ends with choir singing number patterns in German; Robert Brown portrayed the violinist in the original Avignon production

Opera: Anthony Roth Costanzo wore gold leaf on his bald head in the 2019 Met production; solo trumpet frequently accompanies the countertenor; epilogue depicts tourists among ruins as ghosts join a funeral procession
Film score: distributed by Francis Ford Coppola; cinematographer Ron Fricke (who later made Baraka); camera zooms out from cave paintings; satellite photo of LA dissolves into a microchip

Composer: operas include In the Penal Colony ('pocket opera' based on Kafka, libretto by Rudy Wurlitzer), The Voyage (featuring Columbus), Appomattox (end of the Civil War), and Hydrogen Jukebox (title from Ginsberg's 'Howl')
Composer: 20 Piano Etudes' first six written for Dennis Russell Davies's 50th birthday (Maki Namekawa recorded full cycle); Violin Concerto No. 2 replaces cadenzas with 'Prologue' and three 'Songs'

recursive_suggestions: Einstein on the Beach — own deep dive; enormous number of clues in quizbowl
Steve Reich — frequent counterpart to Glass in minimalism questions; Juilliard classmate
Akhnaten — the pharaoh; might come up independently in history/archaeology questions
Koyaanisqatsi — Godfrey Reggio film trilogy worth separate search

second_pass.notes: Added clues from 61 tossup mentions and 58 bonus mentions. Enriched Einstein on the Beach (Robert Brown, knee play organ/counting details, Words Without Music), Akhnaten (Costanzo/Bridges Met production, trumpet, epilogue), Koyaanisqatsi (Coppola, Fricke, Truman Show connection), Satyagraha (DeJong libretto). Added many new works to Other Works: In the Penal Colony, The Voyage, Appomattox, Hydrogen Jukebox, Galileo Galilei, The Photographer, Piano Concerto No. 2, Violin Concerto No. 2, Piano Etudes.
